metadata.title: Quick-fire: Past misrepresentations reactivated
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Companion to qf-truth-001 (in-person counterparty variation) and qf-truth-003 (modality variation). Where those test in-the-moment honesty decisions, this round tests truth-telling-with-time-lag: each item presents a past misrepresentation (white lie, exaggeration, omission, missed deadline-commitment) that has now been reactivated by changed circumstances. Does the user come clean, double down, or let the moment pass? The structure deliberately removes the social-pressure element that drives qf-truth-001 in favor of a more solitary correction-or-not decision.
metadata.domain_anchor: Vrij 2008 on the dynamics of cumulative deception (each unrevealed lie costs increasing cognitive load over time); Mazar/Ariely self-concept maintenance framework (the moment of opportunity to correct is also a moment to consolidate a self-image). Abeler 2019 honesty meta provides population base rates for active deception under stakes.

items.prompt: Last week you told a friend you couldn't make their party. You ended up going to a different one. The party hosts of the second event mention you to your friend, in passing.

options.text: Bring it up first: "Hey, you'll probably hear I was at X's. I should have just told you."
Wait and see. If they bring it up you can address it then.

items.prompt: In a recent job interview you slightly overstated your role on a past project. You got the offer. In your second week your manager is referencing 'the work you did on X' and asking for similar work.

options.text: Clarify: "I should be clear about how much of X was mine vs my team — I want to set the right expectation."
Deliver as if the overstatement were true and adjust as you go.

items.prompt: You told your partner a casual estimate of what you spent on something recently. You underestimated by roughly 30%. The credit card bill is going to arrive in the joint inbox in two days.

options.text: Mention it now: 'Heads up — I undercounted by about $X when I told you the estimate.'
Let them notice if they notice. If asked, explain then.

items.prompt: Three weeks ago you agreed to a project deadline that you privately thought was unrealistic. You didn't push back at the time. You're now halfway in and you can see clearly you won't make it.

options.text: Flag now: 'I should have raised this when we set the deadline. I'm not going to make it; what can we restructure?'
Wait until closer to the deadline; maybe the situation will change and you'll find a way.

items.prompt: A year ago in a brainstorm you mildly took credit for an idea that was actually a coworker's. The coworker is now interviewing for a promotion. You hear that idea has come up positively, attributed to you, in their interview committee's discussion.

options.text: Reach out to the committee chair: 'I want to clarify — that idea was actually [coworker's]. They should get the credit.'
Stay out of it. Bringing it up now would muddy the waters for the coworker and you both.

items.prompt: Six months ago a now-distant family member asked how you were doing. You said 'great' — you were actually struggling. They text again today asking the same.

options.text: Be more honest this time: 'It's been a hard stretch, actually. Can we catch up properly sometime?'
Keep the surface-friendly reply. The distance is real for a reason.
